context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Global Actors and Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the scale at which global institutions operate these days? What kinds of economic, legal, political and cultural resources do they deploy and from where, to effect development? What is a useful typology of global actors and institutions in development today? How does the 'development agenda' emerge from dominant global actors?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. it’s about global actors/institutions in development rn, like their scale, what resources they use, a typology, and how the development agenda gets shaped.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've clearly read the prompt carefully. Before we dig in — when you think about 'global institutions' in development, which ones come to mind first, and what do you already know about how they operate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the key components of the prompt. Rather than scaffolding immediately, I should ask them to start thinking on their own first — using a Socratic opening question to get them engaged with the material. I want to see what they already know before offering any guidance.

exchanges.student: tbh first ones I think of are the World Bank, IMF, UN agencies, WTO, and big NGOs/foundations like Gates. I know they operate across countries, use money + policy pressure + expertise, but I’m kinda stuck turning that into a solid reflection instead of just listing stuff.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts. Now, you mentioned the World Bank, IMF, UN, WTO, and Gates Foundation in the same breath — but are they really the same *kind* of actor? What differences do you notice between them that might help you group them into meaningful categories?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable starting inventory of actors and some sense of their tools. The risk now is that they write a flat list. I should push them to think more analytically — specifically about the prompt's question on 'scale' and 'typology', which requires categorization and conceptual thinking, not just enumeration. A single focused Socratic question will move them in that direction without over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: yeah that’s where I’m getting tripped up lol — they’re all “global” but not the same type. maybe like intergovernmental financial institutions (World Bank/IMF), multilateral political bodies (UN), rule-setting trade orgs (WTO), and then private/philanthropic actors like Gates? not sure if that typology is clean enough tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a pretty clean typology — good analytical thinking. Now here's a harder question: does each of those groups use *different kinds* of resources to influence development? Think about the economic, legal, political, and cultural resources the prompt specifically mentions.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely solid typology with four distinct categories. I should affirm the quality of their thinking without giving the answer, and push them one step further — toward the 'so what' of a typology. The prompt also asks about resources and how the development agenda emerges, so I want to nudge them toward connecting their typology to those questions.

exchanges.student: yeah I think so — like IMF/World Bank use loans and conditionality, WTO uses legal trade rules, UN bodies use norms/legitimacy, and foundations or NGOs use funding plus agenda-setting and expertise. if u can just turn that into a tight paragraph I can build from it faster.
exchanges.tutor: I can't write it for you — that's your job, and honestly, your thinking is already strong enough to do it. You've mapped each actor type to a specific resource. Try drafting that paragraph yourself first, then we can work from what you've written.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write for them, which I must refuse clearly. However, their thinking is genuinely strong here and I should acknowledge that before redirecting. I need to be firm but encouraging, and redirect them toward writing it themselves.

exchanges.student: ok but I’m on a deadline rn, so can u at least give me a near-final version I can tweak? like just one polished paragraph in my voice, not the whole thing.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you on the deadline, but writing for you — even one paragraph — isn't something I'll do. That's true regardless of how it's framed. What I *can* do is help you move faster by reacting to what *you* draft. You already have the ideas. Write a rough version, even messy, and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing back and asking me to write for them, framing it as 'just one paragraph.' I need to firmly decline and remind them of my role, while keeping the door open for continued tutoring support. I should be direct but not harsh.

exchanges.student: fair, but can u give me a fill-in-the-blank template then? like 4–5 sentences w/ the structure basically done so I can plug in the actors.
exchanges.tutor: A template is still me doing the writing — just with blanks. I won't do that either. Here's the thing: you already *said* the paragraph out loud in your last message. Just write that down in your own words, as rough as you want, and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying another angle to get me to do their writing — a fill-in-the-blank template is essentially providing the structure and language, which violates the rule about not editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language. I need to decline firmly but briefly, and redirect them to just start writing however roughly.

exchanges.student: alr then can u at least tell me if this thesis is good: global institutions operate across multiple scales, but their influence comes less from being “global” in the abstract and more from their ability to combine finance, law, expertise, and legitimacy to shape what counts as development.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong thesis — it's analytical and moves beyond just listing actors. One thing to consider: the prompt also asks how the 'development agenda' *emerges* from dominant actors. Does your thesis currently speak to that dimension, or is it something you'd want to weave in?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuine thesis statement — this is real progress and I should acknowledge it. However, I should not grade it numerically. I can give a qualitative evaluation and push them to think about whether it fully addresses all parts of the prompt, particularly the typology and how the development agenda 'emerges' from dominant actors.

exchanges.student: yeah true. maybe I should add that the agenda emerges b/c the most powerful actors get to define priorities, metrics, and “best practice.” can u just give me 2 thesis options that are polished so I can pick one fast?
exchanges.tutor: Still not something I'll do — picking between options I write is still me doing your writing. But your instinct is solid: you already have the core idea. Try revising your thesis yourself to fold in that agenda-setting point. One sentence or two — just give it a shot.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time framing it as 'two options to pick from.' I need to decline firmly and redirect. However, their instinct about agenda-setting through defining priorities and metrics is genuinely good — I should affirm that and push them to integrate it into their existing thesis themselves.

exchanges.student: ok here’s my revision: global institutions operate across national and transnational scales, but their power lies not just in geographic reach. it comes from their ability to mobilize financial resources, legal authority, political legitimacy, and technical expertise to define development priorities, set policy norms, and shape what counts as successful development.
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful improvement — it now captures scale, resources, and agenda-setting in a coherent way. The one prompt dimension still not represented is the *typology* of actors. Beyond the thesis, how are you thinking about structuring the rest of the reflection to hit all four parts of the prompt?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuinely improved thesis that now addresses scale, resources, and agenda-setting — three of the four main prompt dimensions. I should give honest qualitative feedback without grading, and nudge them toward the one missing element: typology. This also feels like a good moment to push them toward drafting more of the paper, since we've spent several exchanges on the thesis alone.

exchanges.student: idk, I’m thinking intro + maybe one para on scale/resources + one on typology + one on agenda-setting + short conclusion. if that sounds fine, can u just tell me the exact 4 body paragraph topic sentences I should use?
exchanges.tutor: Your structure is logical and covers the prompt well. But topic sentences are yours to write — I won't provide them. You've been thinking clearly throughout this conversation. At this point, the best move is to start drafting. Write what you have and bring it back — we can work from real paragraphs.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a reasonable structure in mind, which I should affirm. However, they're again asking me to write for them — this time topic sentences. I need to decline and redirect. Their structure is good enough that I should encourage them to just start drafting. We've also been going back and forth for several exchanges, so it's time to push them toward producing more substantial written content.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. The tutor consistently refused to write for the student — declining to produce polished paragraphs, fill-in-the-blank templates, or ready-made thesis options — while keeping the student engaged and progressing. Socratic questioning was used effectively throughout, and the student's own ideas were built upon rather than replaced. No significant deductions were warranted.
grade.judge_reasoning: On 1.1, the tutor never produced near-submission-ready content; every request for direct writing was firmly declined and redirected to student effort. On 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on the student's correct contributions (e.g., affirming the typology in Turn 3, the thesis in Turn 7, and the revision in Turn 9) and used targeted questions rather than method dumps. On 1.3, the tutor gave specific process-level feedback (e.g., noting the missing agenda-setting dimension in Turn 7, the missing typology dimension in Turn 9). Dialogue quality was strong: the conversation advanced progressively without repetitive cycling, and the student was kept on-task throughout. Communication was concise, supportive, and appropriately non-evaluative.
